nodes.en: Holistic study of human cultures, both past and present, examining material culture, social organization, language, and biological variation across societies.
nodes.cs: Holistické studium lidských kultur, minulých i současných, zkoumající hmotnou kulturu, sociální organizaci a jazykové rozdíly.
nodes.brief:
Anthropology is the holistic study of human cultures, past and present, encompassing material culture, social organization, language, and biological variation across societies. It distinguishes itself from [[04.02]] Sociology by its comparative, often long-durée and cross-cultural approach, and from [[04.01]] Psychology by treating the individual as inseparable from the cultural system that gives their behavior meaning.

Its subfields split along method and evidence: living communities are studied through ethnographic fieldwork, languages through linguistic analysis, human biological variation through physical anthropology, and vanished societies through [[04.10]] Archaeology, which reconstructs past cultures from excavated material remains rather than living informants. The discipline's attention to settlement patterns and cultural distribution also connects it to [[04.08]] Geography, which supplies the spatial framework for interpreting where and why cultures took the forms they did.

Anthropology's four-field tradition, cultural, linguistic, biological, and archaeological, remains a distinguishing feature of the discipline in North America, though the branches operate largely independently elsewhere. The [American Anthropological Association](https://www.americananthro.org/AdvanceYourCareer/Content.aspx?ItemNumber=1971) sets out the field's scope and ethical standards, and the [Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy entry on relativism](https://plato.stanford.edu/entries/relativism/) addresses the methodological debate at the discipline's core.
